items.q: What temperature range is the food 'danger zone'?
items.correct: 5 to 63 degrees Celsius - where most bacteria multiply rapidly.
items.why: Students assume danger = very hot. It's the MIDDLE range that's dangerous because bacteria thrive there. Fridges stay below 5; cooking exceeds 63 (often 75+ for safety). The middle is where they multiply.

items.correct: Proteins denaturing and bonding to form a solid structure when heated.
items.misconception: The egg shell breaking when boiled.
items.why: Students think coagulation is something physical happening to the shell. It's PROTEIN CHEMISTRY: heat unfolds the proteins, which then bond together, turning liquid egg into solid - what makes a fried egg set.

items.q: Are local foods always more sustainable than imported ones?
items.correct: Not always - production methods can outweigh transport emissions.
items.misconception: Yes - shorter distance always means lower emissions.
items.random_wrong: No - imported food is always better.
items.why: Students apply 'shorter is better' too simply. A heated UK greenhouse tomato may have higher emissions than a Spanish field tomato trucked north. Lifecycle analysis matters more than miles alone.

items.q: Order effect is a kind of:
items.why: It's Bias. “Nutrient” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Trained tasters with shared vocabulary. “Random shoppers” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Typical maximum number on a 0-10 sensory rating scale?
items.why: Exact answer: 10. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
